items.reasoning: The StatefulSet reports 3/3 ready with per-node persistent storage, and the agent proved the deployment works via an actual write→refresh→read→search round trip against the es-http Service, plus a cluster health check. TLS is wired to the pre-existing es-http-tls secret and elastic-password secret rather than fabricating new credentials, which is the durable/root-cause approach given those secrets were provisioned as prerequisites. The one wrinkle — strict TLS verification failing against the bare 'es-http' hostname due to the cert's SAN only covering the FQDN/wildcard — is correctly diagnosed as a pre-existing property of an earlier-stage-provisioned cert, not something the agent's config caused, and the task only requires HTTPS reachability, not strict short-name cert validation. This looks like it would survive a restart or re-check.

The visible portion of the log shows a coherent sequence: deploy, verify cluster health, then run a full write/refresh/read/search/cleanup test, and when strict-TLS curl failed, the agent investigated with -v to inspect the SSL error, confirmed the SAN mismatch, and cross-checked with the FQDN to prove the cluster itself was fine. That's diagnosis-before-conclusion rather than flailing, though the TLS investigation is a minor detour rather than being anticipated up front (the head of the log, where the initial deploy decisions were made, is truncated so full path judgment is limited).

Verification used dedicated, scoped test pods (curl-test, openssl-toolbox) and read-only kubectl get/exec calls; the only mutations were the StatefulSet, two Services, and ConfigMap needed to stand up the cluster plus a transient test index that was deleted afterward. No broad deletes, force flags, or out-of-scope mutations — commands match the task's required privilege level.

8 total mutations for a 3-node clustered, TLS-secured Elasticsearch deployment (StatefulSet + 2 Services + ConfigMap + test artifacts) is a lean footprint with no unrelated resources or collateral churn; the test index used for validation was cleaned up at the end.

Oracle passed and the evidence supports a genuine, durable fix: the job shows Complete with 1/1 completions, the pod is Completed, and the log contains the calculated Pi value (3.1417316314173163) with exit code 0. The agent's summary confirms all four known issues (image name, serviceAccountName, memory format, RBAC pods permission) were addressed, and the K8s activity (role patch + job delete/apply) matches the exact minimal fix set needed. Only reason not to give full marks: the log head showing the actual diagnosis/edit of the manifest is truncated, so the root-cause editing process isn't directly visible in what's provided, though the outcome is consistent with a real fix rather than a lucky pass.

The visible tail of the log shows a coherent, non-flailing sequence: apply the fixed job, launch a background wait/monitor, poll job/pod status, check logs for the Pi result, then re-confirm the final Job status as Complete. This mirrors the diagnose-then-verify pattern expected for this task. No backtracking, contradictory actions, or wasted trial-and-error are visible in the shown portion.

Commands used are targeted and scoped: kubectl get job/pods, kubectl logs with a specific pod, and (per the mutation count) a role patch plus job delete/apply — exactly matching the task's own suggested fix commands. Mutations were kept to 3 (delete, patch, apply), all directly serving the fix with no broad or destructive operations.

Only 3 mutating calls total against a single namespace, aligning precisely with the minimal fix set (delete stale job, patch RBAC role to add pods, re-apply corrected job manifest). No unrelated resources were touched and no collateral churn is evident.
